content:
2 min read
Just now

--

A developer I know sent me their side project to look over before launch. Auth worked, signup worked, everything ran. Then I opened the file where they stored passwords and found a single line: hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest(). No salt. No work factor. A raw MD5 digest going straight into the users table.
They had not written that line. Cursor did. And I was not surprised, because I see this constantly. Claude Code does it. Copilot does it. Ask almost any AI editor to hash a password before saving it and there is a real chance you get MD5 or SHA-1 back.
The vulnerable code
import hashlib
# fast hash, no salt, no work factor (CWE-916)
def store_password(password):
return hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()Sometimes it is SHA-1 instead. Sometimes SHA-256. It does not matter which one. They are all designed to be fast, and speed is the exact opposite of what you want when hashing a password.
Why this keeps happening
The training data explains it. MD5 password hashing was standard advice on StackOverflow and in PHP tutorials for the better part of a decade. Millions of examples. The models learned the pattern because the pattern was everywhere. They do not know that in 2012 LinkedIn leaked 6.5 million unsalted SHA-1 passwords and most of them were cracked within days. They only know that hash a password and md5 appear together a lot.
The math is what actually bites you. A modern GPU rig computes billions of MD5 hashes per second. When a user database leaks, and databases leak, every common password falls almost instantly. No salt means two users with the same password get the same hash, so a single rainbow table cracks the whole table at once.
The fix
Use a slow hash that was built for passwords: bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2id. They are deliberately expensive to compute. That cost is invisible on a single login but devastating to an attacker running billions of guesses.
import bcrypt
# salted, slow by design, tuneable work factor
def store_password(password):
return bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))def verify_password(password, stored):
return bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode(), stored)
bcrypt salts automatically, so you never manage salts yourself. rounds=12 is a sane default in 2026. If you want the current best practice, argon2id through the argon2-cffi library is the OWASP-recommended choice.
The trap is that your AI editor will not warn you about the MD5 version. It is valid Python. It runs. It passes your tests. Nothing tells you it is wrong until the breach notification lands.
I have been running SafeWeave for exactly this. It hooks into Cursor and Claude Code as an MCP server and flags weak crypto like md5/sha1 password hashing before I move on to the next file. That said, even a basic pre-commit hook with semgrep will catch most of it. The important thing is catching it while you still have the context, whatever tool you use.
